data.title: Mortgage rates remain in narrow range for fourth straight week
data.description:
Mortgage rates remained within the same narrow range for the fourth consecutive week, the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey showed.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.72% as of July 31, down slightly from 6.74% last week and 6.73% in the same period of 2024.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.85%, down from 5.87% a week ago and 5.99% a year ago.

"Continued economic growth, along with moderating house prices and rising inventory, bodes well for buyers and sellers alike," said Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist.

Here is a look at the mortgage rate movements in the past four weeks:

data.title: Aqua Metals to execute reverse stock split
data.description:
• Aqua Metals (NASDAQ:AQMS) plans to execute a reverse stock split of its common stock at a ratio of 1 post-split share for every 10 pre-split shares.
  • The reverse stock split will become effective at on August 4, 2025. The action will reduce the number of issued shares of the company's common stock from 13,948,763 shares to around 1,394,876 shares.
  • Shares fell nearly 10%.

data.title: Market Voices: Trump on trade deals with Mexico, Canada, South Korea
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements and remarks that could impact markets, sectors or individual stocks. 

  • President Trump announced Thursday morning that trade negotiations with Mexico have been extended for 90 days.

"I have just concluded a telephone conversation with the President of Mexico, Claudia Sheinbaum, which was very successful in that, more and more, we are getting to know and understand each other. The complexities of a Deal with Mexico are somewhat different than other Nations because of both the problems, and assets, of the Border," Trump said in a long post on Truth Social. 

"We have agreed to extend, for a 90 Day period, the exact same Deal as we had for the last short period of time, namely, that Mexico will continue to pay a 25% Fentanyl Tariff, 25% Tariff on Cars, and 50% Tariff on Steel, Aluminum, and Copper. Additionally, Mexico has agreed to immediately terminate its Non Tariff Trade Barriers, of which there were many. We will be talking to Mexico over the next 90 Days with the goal of signing a Trade Deal somewhere within the 90 Day period of time, or longer," Trump added.

  • Meanwhile, Trump said that Canada's announcement that it plans to officially recognize the Palestinian state in September along with the U.K. and France will complicate a trade deal with the U.S.

"Wow! Canada has just announced that it is backing statehood for Palestine. That will make it very hard for us to make a Trade Deal with them. Oh,Canada!!!" Trump said in a post on Truth Social. 

  • Trump also announced a trade deal with South Korea that calls for a 15% tariff on South Korean goods, along with South Korean investments in the U.S. and purchases of energy products.

"The Deal is that South Korea will give to the United States $350 Billion Dollars for Investments owned and controlled by the United States, and selected by myself, as President. Additionally, South Korea will purchase $100 Billion Dollars of LNG, or other Energy products and, further, South Korea has agreed to invest a large sum of money for their Investment purposes. This sum will be announced within the next two weeks when the President of South Korea, Lee Jae Myung, comes to the White House for a Bilateral Meeting," Trump said in a post on Truth Social. 

"It is also agreed that South Korea will be completely OPEN TO TRADE with the United States, and that they will accept American product including Cars and Trucks, Agriculture, etc. We have agreed to a Tariff for South Korea of 15%. America will not be charged a Tariff," Trump added.

  • Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that the U.S. and China are getting closer to signing a trade deal.

“I believe that we have the makings of a deal,” Bessent said during an interview on CNBC. “There’s still a few technical details to be worked out on the Chinese side between us. I’m confident that it will be done, but it’s not 100% done.”

The U.S. and China have until Aug. 12 to agree to a deal before higher tariffs kick in for both countries.

data.title: Ford's 'Model T' moment, capital reallocation from EVs takes pressure off shares
data.description:
Ford (NYSE:F) is clawing back some recent losses as investors reassess its solid Q2 results and the strategic shift of diverting funds from its unprofitable EV unit to its cash cow Ford Pro division.

To accelerate the high-margin and reoccurring revenue of Ford Pro, CEO Jim Farley said on the earnings call, the company will “reallocate the resources on future EV programs to accelerate progress over [the company’s] multiyear business plan…and shift capital towards Pro.”

Farley also announced plans to build a “breakthrough” electric vehicle and platform in the U.S.

“This is a Model T moment for us at Ford,” Farley said of the new low-cost EV, presenting a chance to bring in “a new family of vehicles to the world that offer incredible technology, efficiency, space and features.”

These developments helped offset some of the pressure on shares from anticipated tariff headwinds, but analysts remain cautious that ongoing affordability issues and costs associated with reshoring manufacturing could make it difficult for the company to deliver consistent results through the rest of FY25, especially as warranty issues spill into H2 results. 

Additionally, establishing a profitable EV business in the current environment (expiration of EV tax credits, margin pressures) will become even more problematic after Chinese EV tech migrates into the U.S.

“While we expect the company to put a 'brave face' on their EV strategy…investors remain skeptical for how Ford could operate a profitable EV business when even Tesla’s car business is unprofitable on an underlying basis,” Morgan Stanley’s Adam Jonas says.

Tariffs are also keeping a lid on Ford’s (NYSE:F) profitability, especially after the company said it expects a $2B headwind for 2025, leading to a downgrade in EBIT guidance for the year.

(Note: As we go to press, the U.S. and Mexico just announced a 90-day pause on tariffs, keeping the 25% import tax in place. The news is giving a modest lift to U.S. automakers).  

However, in the event the two nations hammer out a deal before the end of October, Evercore ISI’s Chris McNally believes Ford (NYSE:F) can regain 15 to 30 cents of EPS “as Mexico is the majority of their tariff exposure through 400K of Mustang Mach E, Bronco, and Maverick production.”

Adds BofA Securities’ Federico Merendi, “We think Ford’s liquidity level is sufficient to weather potential challenges from production pressures, future product launches and tariffs.”

After setting a 6-month high at the open, Ford (F) shares have retreated, but are still holding onto a 2% gain from Wednesday’s close. Shares are up 9.6% year-to-date versus +2.4% and -31% for rivals General Motors (GM), and Stellantis (STLA), respectively.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P 500 gets a boost from Microsoft and Meta earnings euphoria
data.description:
Wall Street on Thursday was largely higher, helped by post-earnings euphoria for tech titans Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT). Sentiment was slightly dampened, however, by economic data that showed stubborn inflation and weak personal spending. 

Market participants also kept a close eye on U.S. President Donald Trump's trade announcements ahead of a key tariff deadline.

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last +0.5% in midday trade, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was flat.

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) led the three major averages, +0.9%. The tech-heavy index had risen as much as +1.6%, helped by Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT).   

The Facebook-parent surged +12.2% after impressing investors with blowout quarterly results and a higher-than-anticipated capital expenditure forecast.

Meanwhile, Microsoft (MSFT) climbed +4.5%, becoming just the second company to cross $4T in market capitalization. The Satya Nadella-led firm reported $75B in annual Azure cloud sales.

Big tech will continue to grab the spotlight, with iPhone-maker Apple (AAPL) and e-commerce and tech giant Amazon (AMZN) on tap to report their results after the closing bell.

Countering some of the earnings excitement was Thursday's economic calendar. The core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index - widely seen as the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge - came in a tad hotter-than-anticipated on a Y/Y basis for June. The +2.8% reading remains stubbornly higher than the central bank's desired +2% target. 

The PCE report came as a further blow to hopes for Fed rate cuts, which had already taken a beating on Wednesday following chair Jerome Powell's post-monetary policy decision press conference. Powell signaled that the Fed was in no hurry to cut interest rates, as the full effect of Trump's tariffs had still not been felt. 

"Powell successfully discouraged those hoping for a Sept rate cut yesterday. This morning's relatively hot PCE report just added fuel to that trend," Parker Ross, global chief economist at Arch Capital Group, said on X (formerly Twitter).

According to the CME FedWatch tool, the odds of a 25 basis point rate cut by the Fed in September have further slipped to 39% from about 47% a day ago. 

"Global investors anticipate two rate cuts from the Fed this year and more next year. This is a reasonable forecast and consistent with my baseline, but I don’t hold it with any confidence. It is increasingly likely the Fed will sit on its hands and not change monetary policy in 2025," Mark Zandi, chief economist at Moody's Analytics, said on X.

"Given the mounting tariffs, inflation, which is already meaningfully above the Fed’s 2% target, will significantly accelerate. The core consumer expenditure deflator is up 2.8% over the year through June, and given the tariffs will be closer to 4% by next spring," Zandi added. 

Speaking of tariffs, Friday marks the start of August and, more importantly, a day when Trump's suspension of reciprocal tariffs will end. The trade barriers, first announced on "Liberation Day" on April 2, were pushed back twice, first to July 9 and now to August 1. The U.S. administration is still negotiating with countries, with Trump having announced updates on India, South Korea, and Mexico.

Turning to the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields fell. They had surged the previous day as traders had dumped bonds during Powell's press conference. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last down 4 basis points to 4.34%, while the shorter-end, more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 2 basis points to 3.93%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Xcel Energy outlines $15B+ incremental investment opportunity as infrastructure demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Xcel Energy (XEL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert C. Frenzel reported that "in the second quarter of 2025, Xcel Energy continued to demonstrate our commitment to our customers, investors and communities to make energy work better." He highlighted strong earnings of $0.75 per share for the quarter and noted $2.6 billion invested in energy infrastructure, emphasizing wildfire risk reduction efforts and the company's confidence in delivering on its earnings guidance for the 21st consecutive year.
  • Frenzel stated, "we now believe that we're likely to need an additional $15 billion of capital investment to meet our customer needs, largely within our current 5-year forecast and some beyond," identifying drivers such as a new generation plan for Texas and New Mexico, and major transmission projects in the Upper Midwest.
  • In the SPS region, Frenzel called out strong energy demand from electrification and manufacturing, and reported the June filing of a generation plan including nearly 5,200 megawatts of generation storage by 2030, with over 4,500 megawatts to be company-owned.
  • CFO Brian J. Van Abel stated, "Xcel Energy delivered earnings of $0.75 per share for the second quarter of 2025 compared to earnings of $0.54 per share in the second quarter of 2024." He listed higher revenue from electric and natural gas service, and increased AFUDC as significant earnings drivers, partially offset by higher interest charges, depreciation, and O&M.
  • Van Abel highlighted a 3.5% increase in weather-normalized electric sales for the quarter and reaffirmed the company's full-year sales growth forecast at 3%.

Outlook

  • The company reaffirmed its 2025 earnings guidance range of $3.75 to $3.85 per share, with Van Abel stating, "we remain confident in our ability to deliver long-term earnings growth in the upper half of our 6% to 8% target range."
  • Frenzel indicated that Xcel Energy expects to formally update its 5-year capital plan through 2030 on the third quarter earnings update, with the newly identified $15 billion in incremental capital needs to be clarified between the base and incremental plans at that time.

Financial Results

  • Xcel Energy reported earnings of $0.75 per share for Q2 2025, up from $0.54 per share in Q2 2024.
  • Van Abel identified higher revenue from electric and natural gas service as a key driver, with increased earnings by $0.24 per share, and higher AFUDC contributing $0.07 per share.
  • Offsetting these were higher interest charges (decreasing earnings by $0.04 per share), increased depreciation and amortization (down $0.03), and higher O&M (down $0.02).
  • The company invested $2.6 billion in infrastructure during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Carly S. Davenport, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the conversion of the $15 billion CapEx upside into the base capital plan. Van Abel responded that regulatory filings in New Mexico and Texas are imminent, with commission decisions expected in the first half of next year, and that the company will provide clarity on what is included in the base plan in Q3.
  • Nicholas Joseph Campanella, Barclays: Asked about the impact of treasury orders on renewable build-out and CapEx. Van Abel explained that the company had started physical construction on many projects and feels well positioned, stating, "we feel good at delivering those projects for our customers."
  • Campanella also asked how the CapEx upside will affect rate base and EPS growth. Van Abel noted that equity issuance is managed with a balanced mix of debt and equity, and further guidance will be provided in Q3.
  • Jeremy Bryan Tonet, JPMorgan: Questioned how competitive transmission opportunities are incorporated into plans. Van Abel clarified, "We don't include them in our capital plan unless they're one."
  • Tonet also inquired about data center contracting progress. Frenzel reported 1.1 gigawatts under construction and a robust pipeline, aiming for about 2.5% by 2030.
  • Sophie Ksenia Karp, KeyBanc: Asked about potential asset sales as alternatives to equity issuance. Van Abel responded, "we're not all that interested in minority interest sales," emphasizing that asset sales would only be considered for strategic reasons.
  • Steven Isaac Fleishman, Wolfe Research: Asked about federal land issues for renewable projects. Van Abel confirmed, "We don't have any projects on federal land."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed interest in growth opportunities but raised questions on regulatory timing, CapEx conversion, treasury guidance, and wildfire litigation, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, with frequent affirmations such as "we feel really good about this" and "we believe we have one of the best growth prospects in the industry."
  • Compared to the prior quarter, analyst tone remains inquisitive but more focused on the magnitude of CapEx and its integration into the base plan, while management's confidence has increased, emphasizing preparedness and growth potential.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a significant increase in incremental capital investment needs, rising from a $10 billion-plus pipeline last quarter to $15 billion highlighted this quarter.
  • Management's tone shifted from a focus on tariff and regulatory risks to a more assertive stance on growth and infrastructure investment, signaling increased confidence in demand projections and the company's ability to execute.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from regulatory and tariff risk to specifics of capital plan updates and growth conversion.
  • Guidance language and EPS range remain unchanged, but management now signals greater visibility and ambition regarding future capital investment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing wildfire risk and extreme weather, with continued investments in mitigation and recent regulatory approvals for wildfire and resiliency plans in Colorado and Texas.
  • Van Abel reported progress in resolving wildfire claims, with $123 million paid out of $176 million in settlements through Q2 2025, reaffirming the low end of the estimated liability at $290 million, which remains below insurance coverage.
  • The upcoming Marshall trial was noted, with management maintaining that the company's equipment did not cause the second ignition.
  • Regulatory timing and approval remain a risk for planned capital investments, especially the $15 billion incremental forecast.

Final Takeaway

Xcel Energy is positioning itself for accelerated infrastructure growth, with management highlighting a potential $15 billion in incremental investment needs driven by robust demand for electrification, manufacturing, and data centers. The company remains confident in its ability to deliver long-term earnings growth, is reaffirming 2025 EPS guidance, and continues to make progress on wildfire risk mitigation. Regulatory approvals and successful execution of the expanded capital plan will be central to meeting these ambitions and maintaining strong financial performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lincoln Electric raises 2025 sales growth outlook with Alloy Steel acquisition and targets $60M in cost savings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lincoln Electric Holdings, Inc. (LECO) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Steven B. Hedlund, CEO, reported "strong second quarter results" with 7% sales growth, citing "diligent price management, benefits from our M&A strategy and improved volume performance from the Americas Welding and Harris Products Group segments." He emphasized that "earnings expanded in the quarter with adjusted earnings per share up 11% to $2.60" and noted over 100% cash conversion of free cash flow year-to-date.
  • Hedlund announced that Lincoln Electric "expect[s] to close our acquisition of the remaining 65% interest in Alloy Steel" and stated, "they are a great addition with a strong business that will be accretive to margins and earnings on day 1."
  • CEO Hedlund highlighted that the company achieved "an approximate 3% increase in organic sales in the quarter, led by pricing actions taken to mitigate higher input costs," while mentioning that automation sales have stabilized around $215 million per quarter and backlog remains steady.
  • Gabriel Bruno, CFO, stated, "Our second quarter sales increased 6.6% to $1.089 billion from 5.2% higher price, a 3% benefit from acquisitions and 70 basis points from favorable foreign exchange translation. These increases were partially offset by 2.3% lower volumes."
  • Bruno further reported, "Gross profit dollars increased approximately 6% to $406 million and gross profit margin held relatively steady at 37.3%." He noted, "adjusted operating income increased approximately 10% to $195 million" and "adjusted operating income margin increased 50 basis points to 17.9%."

Outlook

  • Management raised its operating framework assumptions for 2025, expecting "low single-digit percent organic sales growth for the full year" and acquisitions to generate "approximately 270 basis points in sales growth this year with Alloy Steel contributing $20 million to $25 million in sales for the balance of the year."
  • CFO Bruno specified, "we are now including $0.07 of EPS contribution from the Alloy Steel acquisition for the balance of the year."
  • The company is "continuing to target a neutral price cost position" and expects to achieve approximately $60 million in savings from the ongoing 6-quarter program by year-end.
  • Full-year adjusted operating income margin is expected to be "steady to slightly up versus the prior year period with a high teens percent incremental margin."

Financial Results

  • Lincoln Electric reported second quarter sales of $1.089 billion and adjusted EPS of $2.60.
  • Gross profit margin was 37.3%, with a gross profit of $406 million.
  • Adjusted operating income reached $195 million, with an adjusted operating income margin of 17.9%.
  • Cash flows from operations in the first half increased approximately 9% with a 104% conversion ratio.
  • Return on invested capital was reported at 21.7%.
  • The company invested $57 million in growth and returned $169 million to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley: Asked about order trends and customer demand. CFO Bruno responded that "July order trends hold" and noted "more strength in general industries" while remaining cautious on heavy industries and automotive.
  • Castillo also inquired about Harris segment volume dynamics. CFO Bruno explained that removing initial stocking for a new retail customer would result in "more flattish type of volumes progressively as we go into the third quarter."
  • Saree Boroditsky, Jefferies: Asked about customer behavior in response to tariff clarity. CEO Hedlund stated, "as the Trump administration starts to provide some clarity around the tariff rates... that will really go a long way towards addressing the wait-and-see attitude that a lot of our customers have taken."
  • Boroditsky also asked about permanent cost savings. CFO Bruno detailed, "continued focus on evolving the operating model... looking at facilities and shaping efficiencies for the long term."
  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: Asked about automation quoting activity and demand inflection. CEO Hedlund said, "the market situation is very encouraging, and we're optimistic about the future with a conservative assumption that the business is... steady through the second half."
  • Mircea Dobre, Baird: Questioned automation and general industries trends. CEO Hedlund clarified, "our outlook for automation in the second half of the year is steady from the first half" and noted positive trends in consumables and industrial distribution.
  • Nathan Jones, Stifel: Sought details on consumables pricing and volume. CFO Bruno stated, "pricing is a little bit higher on the consumable side... volumes held on the consumables side of our business to be flattish."
  • Steve Barger, KeyBanc: Asked about third quarter seasonality. CFO Bruno replied, "progressing in our normal seasonality is a good assumption."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on order trends, customer demand, and the impact of tariffs. Questions were fact-based and sought clarity on volume sustainability and pricing actions.
  • Management's tone was confident during prepared remarks, with CEO Hedlund emphasizing "strong profit performance" and CFO Bruno discussing "diligent cost management." During the Q&A, management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone, using phrases like "we are optimistic about the future with a conservative assumption" and "we'll respond accordingly... to maintain the neutral price/cost model."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's language in Q2 was more optimistic, raising the full-year outlook and expressing greater confidence in demand stabilization, while analysts' tone shifted from cautious to more constructive, reflecting improved visibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its full-year organic sales growth outlook from "relatively flat" to "low single-digit percent organic sales growth" and increased its acquisition growth contribution projection with the Alloy Steel deal.
  • Guidance language shifted from caution regarding volume declines and tariff headwinds to more constructive commentary on volume resilience and price realization.
  • Key metrics improved: adjusted EPS rose from $2.16 in Q1 to $2.60 in Q2, and sales increased from $1.04 billion to $1.089 billion.
  • Management reinstated merit increases previously paused in Q1 and highlighted improved year-to-date cash flow and ROIC.
  • Analysts in Q2 focused on sustainability and margin outlook, while in Q1, questions centered on uncertainty and risk to the back half of the year.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified continued customer deferral of capital spending due to policy uncertainty, especially impacting equipment and automation portfolios.
  • Weakness in EMEA and Asia Pacific demand, especially outside core Europe and due to project timing.
  • Heavy industries remain challenged, and agricultural machinery OEM destocking persists.
  • Management is mitigating risks through price/cost management, supply chain initiatives to maximize domestic supply, and ongoing operational savings programs.

Final Takeaway

Lincoln Electric delivered strong results in the second quarter, raising its full-year outlook for organic sales growth and integrating the Alloy Steel acquisition, which is expected to be accretive to both margins and earnings. Cost savings initiatives remain on track, with the company targeting $60 million in realized savings by year-end. While customer investment decisions continue to be influenced by policy uncertainty, management remains confident in its strategies for profitable growth and operational efficiency, positioning the business for further improvement as end markets recover.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SPS Commerce falls after 'good results' overshadowed by expectations for 2025-26, says Loop
data.description:
Shares of SPS Commerce (NASDAQ:SPSC) tumbled about 19% on Thursday following management's more cautious outlook for 2026, according to analysts.

Loop Capital downgraded the shares of SPS Commerce — which provides cloud-based supply chain management solutions — to Hold from Buy with a $120 price target.

Analyst Mark Schappel said that SPS's second quarter 2025 results were about as expected with revenue, EPS, and Adjusted EBITDA all surpassing Loop's estimates as well as the high end of guidance.

"However, these reasonably good results were overshadowed by fading expectations for the second half of 2025 and management’s more cautious outlook for 2026, which fell meaningfully short of our projections and the Street consensus," said the analyst.

Schappel noted that the revised guidance reflects elongated sales cycles with new small and medium-sized business, or SMB, customers and upsells, largely driven by increased market uncertainty surrounding tariffs and consumer spending.

The analyst added that they commend management's focus on pivoting to margin expansion in response to mounting growth headwinds, but expect the stock will trade sideways for some time until there is a clear inflection point in demand.

"Normally, we would wait out the mediocre guide in preparation for a bounce, but at this stage we have too many questions. Thus, we drop to a Hold rating until there is greater macro stability and assurance that financial estimates properly account for the impact of ongoing uncertainty," said Schappel.
